JavaScript Core (51) - Inheritance & the Prototype Chain: Multi-Level Inheritance with Object.create
JavaScript Core
Inheritance & Prototype Chain

In this chapter we'll build our own prototype chain from scratch. We'll start with a simple constructor, see why it falls short, then use Object.create to set up multi-level inheritance — Dog inheriting from Animal. Along the way we'll cover why you still need call() to inherit the constructor, and why you have to put back Dog.prototype.constructor to keep the prototype chain intact.

JavaScript Core (50) - Inheritance & the Prototype Chain: Primitive Wrapper Objects and Their Link to Prototypes
Inheritance and the Prototype Chain

Next up we'll look at the relationship between primitive values and their wrapper objects. If you can get these two straight in your head, you'll actually run into a lot fewer bugs. This post walks through how primitives borrow methods from wrapper objects, how that ties back to prototypes, and why you can extend things like String and Date through their prototype.

JavaScript Core Concepts (49): Inheritance & Prototype Chain — Building Your Own Prototype with a Constructor

Time to roll up our sleeves and build a custom prototype using a constructor function. We'll see how `new` spins up new objects that share the same prototype, why `bark` has to live on `prototype` instead of inside the constructor, and how the spot where you attach `prototype` decides whether your method even works. Plus the difference between `__proto__` and `prototype`.

JavaScript Core Concepts (48) — Inheritance & Prototype Chain: Where Does the Prototype Live?

Starting this chapter we finally get into prototypes for real. We'll look at why JavaScript objects inherit from prototypes, what the prototype chain actually is, how methods get shared across instances, and why you should never add methods through __proto__ in real code.

JavaScript Core Concepts (47): Why Does JavaScript Even Have Prototypes?

Time to dig into what a prototype actually is in JavaScript. We'll start with a bit of history — why JavaScript got named the way it did, how it relates to Java, and why that history is the reason JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance instead of class-based inheritance. By the end you'll understand where prototypes come from and why the language needs them.

JavaScript Core Concepts (43) - Functions and How This Works - this and the DOM

Let's look at how this behaves when you're working with the DOM. Whether you bind a handler inline in your HTML or with addEventListener, this points at the element itself - which is a classic gotcha. And if you want it pointing somewhere else, bind is the tool to reach for.
